Title: Man killed as motorcycle bursts into flames in crash
Article:
PUTATAN: A 47-year-old man was killed after his motorcycle caught fire in a crash involving a car along Jalan Putatan bound for Lokawi, early this morning.
The victim was confirmed dead at the scene due to severe injuries.
Putatan Fire and Rescue Station chief Musran Jabri said the department received an emergency call at 3.04am.
He said five firefighters and a fire engine were deployed to the scene, about 5km from the station.
"Upon arrival, the team found a motorcycle 100 per cent engulfed in flames after it is believed to have been struck by a Perodua Viva," he said in a statement today.
Firefighters extinguished the blaze using a hose line from the fire engine.
The victim was pronounced dead at the scene, and his remains were handed over to police for further action.
Checks were carried out to ensure there were no remaining hazards or fire risks at the location.
"After the area was confirmed safe, operations were terminated at 4.48am," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AirBorneo files planned B737-800 flights to KL, Singapore from late July
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: AirBorneo Airways Sdn Bhd has filed planned Boeing B737-800 jet operations in the OAG airline schedules database with proposed services from Kuching to Kuala Lumpur and Singapore Changi from late July, according to aviation route updates platform AeroRoutes.
AeroRoutes reported that the planned jet operation would see the Sarawak-owned airline commence Kuching-Kuala Lumpur services from July 20, and Kuching-Singapore Changi services from July 27, at the earliest.
The planned aircraft configuration is currently shown as C12Y150, which typically indicates a 162-seat layout comprising 12 business class seats and 150 economy class seats.
The schedules remain subject to further changes.
The planned Kuching-Kuala Lumpur schedule shows an afternoon rotation from July 20, with flight MY5005 departing Kuching at 1pm and arriving in Kuala Lumpur at 2.50pm.
The return flight MY5004 is scheduled to depart Kuala Lumpur at 3.35pm and arrive in Kuching at 5.25pm.
A second daily rotation, MY5003/MY5002, is scheduled to operate from July 21.
MY5003 is shown departing Kuching at 7.10am and arriving in Kuala Lumpur at 9am, while MY5002 is scheduled to depart Kuala Lumpur at 9.45am and arrive in Kuching at 11.35am.
For Singapore, the planned schedule shows flight MY363 departing Kuching at 6.50pm and arriving at Singapore Changi at 8.25pm from July 27, 2026.
The return flight MY364 is scheduled to leave Singapore Changi at 9.15pm and arrive in Kuching at 10.50pm.
Business Times has contacted AirBorneo for comment.
The filing comes as Sarawak Premier Tan Sri Abang Johari Tun Openg said AirBorneo was expected to announce its new fare structure either next week or by mid-July with the Kuala Lumpur-Kuching sector among the routes covered.
He said the new fare packages would include baggage allowance and in-flight meals, adding that fares on the Kuala Lumpur-Kuching route would be cheaper in line with the state government's earlier pledge to provide more affordable air travel options.
AirBorneo also teased new destinations on its official Facebook page on July 1 with a post carrying the words "New destinations will drop" and asking users to guess where the airline was flying next.
The post included blurred visuals resembling several Kuala Lumpur landmarks, including the Batu Caves staircase, the large wau installation near Central Market/Pasar Seni, the Petronas Twin Towers and Merdeka 118.
The teaser appeared to point to Kuala Lumpur as one of AirBorneo's upcoming destinations.
The flight schedule filing follows earlier plans by AirBorneo to introduce jet operations as part of its next phase of growth following the Sarawak government's takeover of MASwings Sdn Bhd from Malaysia Aviation Group.
Business Times reported in March that AirBorneo was expected to receive three B737-800 aircraft by July from Ascend Airways Malaysia as it prepared to start jet operations in the third quarter of this year.
The planned jet services are also in line with earlier comments by AirBorneo chief executive officer Megat Ardian Wira Mohd Aminuddin, who told local media in March that the airline planned to launch regional jet operations from Kuching in July using three B737-800 aircraft.
He said at the time that the airline's initial jet routes were expected to connect Kuching with Kuala Lumpur, Singapore and Kota Kinabalu.
Megat Ardian also said the aircraft would be introduced through a wet-lease arrangement while AirBorneo completed the necessary regulatory approvals and operational preparations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 400,000 Year Four pupils to sit first Learning Matrix Assessment in October
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: About 400,000 Year Four pupils are expected to sit the inaugural Year Four Learning Matrix assessment over three days from Oct 6 to 8.
Education Ministry Examinations Board (LP) director Ab Aziz Mamat said the assessment would be conducted simultaneously nationwide, with each subject comprising a written paper lasting one hour and 15 minutes.
The assessment will cover four core subjects — Bahasa Melayu, English, Mathematics and Science — as well as Chinese or Tamil for pupils in national-type schools.
Pupils with special learning needs in mainstream schools will be given an additional 30 to 60 minutes.
Ab Aziz said schools were responsible for registering all Year Four pupils to ensure sufficient question papers were prepared and no candidate was left out.
The assessment instruments were developed by the LP with classroom teachers, based on the Curriculum and Assessment Standard Document covering Year One to Year Four.
The papers comprise objective and subjective questions of varying formats and difficulty levels to assess knowledge, application, problem-solving, reasoning and Higher Order Thinking Skills (Hots).
He said international assessment frameworks, including the Southeast Asia Primary Learning Metrics (SEA-PLM), Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study (Timss) and the Programme for International Student Assessment (Pisa), were referenced to align pupils with global standards.
"To ensure public confidence, the assessment is centrally administered by the LP. LP will coordinate the printing of question papers, which will then be distributed to secure storage facilities nationwide. This ensures all schools receive the same question papers right up to the examination centres," he added.
Pupils under the Dual Language Programme will receive bilingual Science and Mathematics papers, while others will sit the examinations in Bahasa Melayu.
Answer scripts will be marked by the pupils' own subject teachers using LP marking schemes, with moderation and verification processes to ensure consistency.
"This allows teachers to better understand their pupils' strengths and weaknesses while marking the scripts," he said.
Ab Aziz said the grading and scoring system would follow that of the Final Academic Session Examination. However, the results would not be used for admission into fully residential schools, Mara Junior Science Colleges or other specialised institutions, which will continue to use the Special School Entry Assessment.
In January, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim announced the Malaysia Learning Matrix as part of the National Education Blueprint 2026-2035, with implementation beginning for Year Four pupils in 2026 and Form Three students in 2027.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pakistan delegation at Khamenei funeral draws effusive praise from Iran
Article:
HYDERABAD: Pakistan's high-ranking delegation at the funeral of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has won Iran's effusive praise, with the Iranian ambassador to Islamabad praying for "enduring friendship and brotherhood" between the two countries.
Dignitaries and officials from about 100 countries, including Malaysia, Bangladesh, Saudi Arabia, Turkiye, Qatar, Oman, Egypt, Russia, Iraq, China and India, were in Teheran on Friday to pay their respects to Khamenei, who lost his life, along with several family members, in the United States (US)-Israeli attack on his compound in the Iranian capital on Feb 28.
"I am profoundly honoured to announce that Pakistan participated in this solemn ceremony at the highest possible level," Ambassador Reza Amiri Moghadam said in a post on his social media platform X page.
He said the Pakistani delegation was also the largest from any country at the ceremony, where the flag-draped coffins of Khamenei and his family members were placed on a stage.
Pakistan Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif led the delegation, which included Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, Chief of Army Staff Field Marshal Syed Asim Munir, Pakistan People's Party (PPP) chairman Bilawal Bhutto Zardari, National Assembly Speaker Sardar Ayaz Sadiq, Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi and Information Minister Attaullah Tarar.
"The presence of such a distinguished, high-ranking and numerous delegation stands as a testament to the enduring friendship, mutual respect, and fraternal bonds between our two brotherly nations," Moghadam said.
"This meaningful and historical gesture reflects the depth of the historic ties and shared values that have united Iran and Pakistan through the trials and triumphs of history," he added.
Shehbaz paid homage to Khamenei, saying that his "wisdom, leadership and profound influence on Iran and the wider region will be remembered for generations."
Khamenei was Iran's Supreme Leader from 1989 until his assassination at the age of 86.
Iran began public funeral ceremonies on Saturday, with his burial being scheduled for July 9 in Mashhad, the city of Khamenei's birth. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ukrainian capital Kyiv under missile attack, officials say
Article:
KYIV: The Ukrainian capital Kyiv came under a Russian missile attack early on Monday and officials said residents were trapped inside a badly damaged building near the city centre.
Reuters witnesses reported a series of explosions in and around the capital and said air defences were also in action against Russian drones.
Mayor Vitali Klitschko, writing on Telegram, said a residential building had been hit in the historic Podil district.
"People are trapped on the seventh to ninth floors," Klitschko said.
He said drone debris had fallen on a second residential building in the same area and in other districts of the city of 3 million.
At least 30 people were killed in Kyiv on Thursday when Russia launched hundreds of drones and dozens of missiles at the city. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens firmer against greenback as traders reassess US rate hike prospects
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened firmer against the US dollar on Monday as traders reassessed the likelihood of a United States (US) interest rate hike following a weaker-than-expected US nonfarm payrolls (NFP) data.
At 8 am, the local note rose to 4.0695/0780 against the greenback from 4.0690/0735 at Friday's close.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the US Federal Reserve (Fed) chair's reluctance to provide forward guidance on interest rates also supported sentiment towards the ringgit.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: ROI-OPEC+ to boost crude output, but can it deliver and who will buy?
Article:
LAUNCESTON, Australia: The decision by OPEC+ to increase crude oil production quotas for a fifth straight month from August raises two questions.
Can they actually ship the increased output and if they can, who will buy it?
The seven core members of OPEC+, which groups together OPEC and allied producers such as Russia, agreed at a meeting on Sunday to lift quotas by 188,000 barrels per day from August, taking the total increase to almost 800,000 bpd since April.
The first question will be answered in the positive if the Strait of Hormuz can remain open and volumes through the narrow waterway can recover to levels close to before the United States and Israel attacked Iran on February 28.
As an aside, it's worth noting that total crude export volumes from the Middle East should be the benchmark, rather than just flows through the strait.
This is because Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates are continuing to use ports outside the Strait of Hormuz.
But even looking at total shipments from the Middle East, it's clear that while volumes have lifted since the United States and Iran agreed to a 60-day ceasefire on June 17, they are still well below pre-war levels.
June exports were 9.62 million bpd, roughly half of the 18.4 million bpd average for the three months leading up to the Iran conflict, according to data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler.
July is likely to see a further improvement with Kpler tracking shipments of 9.99 million bpd, but this figure is likely to be revised higher as more cargoes are assessed.
But even so, the data show that Middle East exports are still constrained and increased shipments from other regions, such as the Americas and Africa, haven't been enough to offset the losses from the Gulf region.
However, the oil industry has a proven track record of adapting rapidly and it would be a reasonable assumption that they are capable of boosting production and shipments from the Middle East, as long as the Strait of Hormuz stays open.
The crude oil futures market is pricing as if OPEC+ is going to be able to deliver on its higher output quotas, and that there will also be additional crude available from former OPEC+ member the United Arab Emirates, as well as from Iran.
Brent contracts were trading around $71.72 a barrel in early Asian trade on Monday, down from the close of $72.12 on July 3 and also below the $72.48 finish on February 27, the day before the U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran.
SUPPLY GLUT
The crude futures market appears to be pricing for a return to the oversupply narrative that prevailed prior to the Iran war.
If supply chains can be restored and OPEC+ and non-OPEC producers are able to deliver increased production, then this narrative is justified.
Apart from the obvious risk of a return to some form of conflict between the United States and Iran, there are also other factors that may come into play.
Part of the reason that Brent prices did not surge any higher than $126 a barrel during the Iran war was that China, the world's biggest crude importer, dramatically cut back on purchases.
China's seaborne imports dropped to the lowest in more than a decade in June, with Kpler assessing arrivals of 5.84 million bpd, or about half of pre-war levels.
The buying strike seems set to continue in July, with Kpler tracking imports of just 5.31 million bpd, although this figure will be revised higher as more cargoes for July arrival are assessed.
The question is when will China return to the crude market, and if past experience is any guide, it will be when refiners deem the crude price to have fallen far enough.
China has a strong track record of boosting imports when prices fall, and cutting back when they rise, with the pattern being supercharged during the past few months.
It's likely that smaller, independent refiners will return to the market before the major state-owned players, and by August it would not be a surprise to see imports from these plants increase.
If prices do remain weak, then China's larger refiners are also likely to resume purchases, although it will take until at least the fourth quarter for these to show up in import data.
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teheran airspace to remain closed on monday for khamenei's funeral procession
Article:
ISTANBUL: Iran will fully shut Teheran's airspace on Monday as part of funeral arrangements for slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Anadolu Ajansi citing ISNA news agency reported.
Flights are continuing nationwide without restrictions on Sunday, but Tehran's airspace will be completely closed on Monday during the main funeral procession.
Under the schedule announced by the Civil Aviation Organisation, regular flights at Teheran's Mehrabad International Airport and Imam Khomeini International Airport will be suspended on Monday as public ceremonies take place in the capital.
On Tuesday, Mehrabad airport will resume normal operations, while Imam Khomeini airport will remain closed.
Authorities said that on July 9, airspace over the northeastern city of Mashhad and operations at Shahid Hasheminejad International Airport will also be fully suspended during the final burial ceremony.
Flights across the rest of the country will continue without restrictions on July 7 to 8, except for Mashhad on the final day of ceremonies.
Khamenei was killed in a joint US-Israeli attack on Iran on Feb 28, triggering weeks of war and escalating tensions across the region.
The funeral ceremonies officially began Friday, with foreign leaders and official delegations from across the region and beyond paying tribute to the late Iranian supreme leader.
According to the official schedule, public farewell ceremonies will be followed by the main funeral procession in Teheran on Monday.
The funeral rites will then move to Qom on July 7.
On July 8, ceremonies are scheduled in Baghdad, Najaf and Karbala, where the body will be received by religious and political figures before being transferred to major Shia shrines.
The final funeral and burial ceremony is scheduled for July 9 at the Imam Reza Shrine, one of Iran's major Shia holy sites. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health insurance rules, EPF retirement savings among Dewan Rakyat's focus today
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Measures to strengthen health insurance regulations, boost retirement savings for Employees Provident Fund (EPF) contributors and enhance volleyball talent development are among the key issues set to be raised in the Dewan Rakyat today.
According to the Order Paper on the Parliament portal, during Question Time, Tan Kok Wai (PH-Cheras) will ask the Finance Minister about measures to strengthen insurance regulations to protect policyholders, particularly those with critical illnesses and cancer, from policy cancellations and unfair claim rejections, while improving transparency and dispute resolution.
Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun (PH-Port Dickson) will also ask the Finance Minister to outline the government's strategy to ensure Malaysians have adequate retirement savings, particularly EPF contributors, amid rising living costs and an ageing population.
Zakri Hassan (PN-Kangar) will ask the Youth and Sports Minister about efforts to identify and develop talent in indoor and beach volleyball.
Hassan Saad (PN-Baling) will seek an explanation from the Communications Minister on the effectiveness of the National Information Dissemination Centres (Nadi) in improving the socio-economic well-being of rural communities, despite gaps in internet access, digital literacy and marketing capabilities among small entrepreneurs.
Also on the agenda are questions on the effectiveness of the Mobile eCOSS application, introduced in May 2025, in curbing leakages involving subsidised packet cooking oil, and financing schemes for micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs).
The House will then receive a briefing from the chairman of the Health Select Committee on its report on reforming the national organ donation and transplant system.
Members will also continue the committee-stage debate and winding-up on the Competition (Amendment) Bill 2026, followed by the second reading of the Competition Commission (Amendment) Bill 2026.
The Second Meeting of the Fifth Session of the 15th Parliament is scheduled to sit for 16 days and conclude on July 16.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bluesify targets becoming Asean's most trusted cybersecurity provider
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Ekuinas-backed Bluesify Solutions Sdn Bhd is ramping up investments in artificial intelligence (AI), regional expansion and cybersecurity infrastructure as it targets becoming Southeast Asia's most trusted managed security service provider in the next three to five years.
The cybersecurity firm is also establishing a second managed detection and response (MDR) centre in Cyberjaya, which is expected to commence operations in August to support rising demand for round-the-clock cybersecurity services across the region.
Managing director Izman Haidi Mohd Ibrahim said the company aims to build a reputation for anticipating and neutralising increasingly sophisticated cyber threats while strengthening its presence across Asean.
"Regionally, our ambition is to establish a strong presence in key Asean markets by replicating the operational excellence and trust we've built at home.
"We will achieve this by tailoring our solutions to local market needs, building in-country teams, and forming strategic alliances to accelerate adoption," he told Business Times in an email interview.
Izman said Malaysia's cybersecurity landscape is defined by rapid modernisation in response to an increasingly sophisticated and evolving threat environment.
"Organisations across the country are strengthening their cybersecurity posture through increased investment, enhanced capabilities and compliance with new legislation, as they respond to AI-driven cyber threats targeting both critical infrastructure and private citizens.
"We are witnessing growing demand for cybersecurity services not only from large corporations, but also from SMEs and state-owned government-linked companies."
Ekuinas invested in Bluesify through its Dana Asas 1 fund on March 17 last year.
Following the investment, Izman said the company has accelerated several initiatives outlined in its strategic roadmap, including expanding its cybersecurity capabilities and scaling its regional footprint.
He said the new Cyberjaya facility will be linked to Bluesify's main operations centre and significantly strengthen its ability to provide 24/7 cybersecurity detection and response services.
Bluesify is also enhancing transparency for customers through the rollout of its proprietary Detect and Respond platform.
"In line with our commitment to transparency and customer trust, we have launched our proprietary Detect and Respond platform.
"Access to the platform is being progressively rolled out to all managed service customers, providing real-time visibility into security operations, incidents and service performance.
"This initiative reflects our core belief that trust is built through transparency, empowering customers to not only rely on our expertise but also gain direct insight into the services we deliver on their behalf," he said.
Izman added that the company is also advancing its AI-assisted security services and has introduced an AI security initiative as part of its next phase of growth.
Bluesify's clients include leading financial services providers, energy and infrastructure operators, government agencies and statutory bodies - many of whom are designated as part of the National Critical Information Infrastructure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SME credit risk rises as cost pressures intensify
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Credit risk among Malaysia's small and medium enterprises (SMEs) is rising as businesses grapple with persistent cost pressures, raising concerns over cash flows and debt-servicing capacity.
SME Association of Malaysia national president Dr Chin Chee Seong said the increase reflects mounting financial strain rather than irresponsible borrowing behaviour.
"From the feedback we have received from our members, there is no doubt that credit risk among SMEs has increased compared to previous years.
"This does not mean that SMEs are becoming irresponsible borrowers, but rather that many are operating under much greater financial pressure," he told Business Times.
A recent survey by the association showed that 83.3 per cent of SMEs reported operating cost increases of more than 10 per cent, while 63.7 per cent experienced increases exceeding 30 per cent.
Only 9.8 per cent of respondents described their cash flow as healthy, while 49 per cent said they were already under pressure and may require financing support.
Malaysia's SMEs, which account for nearly 97 per cent of business establishments and around 40 per cent of gross domestic product (GDP), have been operating in a persistently challenging environment marked by elevated costs and uneven demand recovery.
While the economy has rebounded from the Covid-19 pandemic, businesses continue to face higher structural expenses. This includes wages, utilities, logistics and financing costs.
The sector is also sensitive to global uncertainties, particularly for export-oriented industries such as electrical and electronics and manufacturing, which remain exposed to fluctuations in external demand and supply chain disruptions.
Chin noted that the squeeze is being driven by rising wages, utilities, rental costs, financing expenses, supply chain disruptions and weaker consumer demand, all of which have compressed profit margins and increased reliance on bank borrowing for working capital.
He said most SMEs are still servicing loans responsibly, but prolonged economic strain could gradually increase credit risks for the banking sector if cash flow pressures persist.
Sectors facing the most stress include manufacturing, logistics and transportation, food and beverage, retail and wholesale trade, as well as export-oriented industries such as electrical and electronics, furniture, plastics, machinery and rubber-based products.
He said these industries are particularly exposed to rising input and energy costs, shipping expenses, labour pressures and weaker demand conditions in both domestic and global markets.
While Malaysia's banking sector has exposure to these cyclical industries due to their importance to the economy, Chin said the key concern is the potential tightening of credit conditions if lenders become more cautious.
He stressed the need for banks to continue distinguishing between fundamentally viable SMEs facing temporary pressure and those with structural weaknesses, and to support viable businesses through restructuring and flexible repayment arrangements where appropriate.
Universiti Teknologi Mara Business Management Faculty senior lecturer Dr Mohamad Idham Md Razak said SMEs are increasingly becoming a key source of credit risk, particularly in the current environment of margin compression, softer demand and elevated operating costs.
"Compared to larger corporates, SMEs typically have thinner financial buffers, lower pricing power and greater sensitivity to cost shocks such as wages, rent and imported inputs.
"As financing costs remain relatively high, debt servicing capacity is also being tested.
"While not yet systemic, the risk profile of SMEs is clearly rising, especially among those in low-margin, highly competitive segments," he added.
Mohamad Idham noted that the most visible signs of stress are emerging in consumer-facing sectors such as retail, food and beverage, and services sensitive to discretionary spending.
He added that parts of the construction and property-related supply chains are also facing pressure due to slower project rollouts and cautious sentiment.
"Export-oriented manufacturing, particularly in electronics, may also see uneven demand recovery.
"In response, banks are generally becoming more cautious, with selective increases in provisions and tighter credit assessments for higher-risk segments, although overall provisioning levels remain manageable at this stage," he said.
In terms of banks, he said Malaysian lenders do have exposure to sectors vulnerable to external shocks, but this exposure is diversified and relatively well managed.
He noted that export manufacturing is sensitive to global demand cycles, commodities are tied to price volatility, and property depends on domestic sentiment and interest rates.
However, he said regulatory oversight, prudent lending standards and generally strong capital buffers have helped contain systemic risk.
"The key concern is not concentration risk per se, but rather a broad-based slowdown that could simultaneously affect multiple sectors, thereby increasing the probability of credit deterioration across banks' loan portfolios," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sex offender list should be 'tiered model'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Malaysia should review access to its  sex offender registry and introduce a tiered, risk-based disclosure system to better protect children and other vulnerable groups, said  criminologist Datuk Dr P. Sundramoorthy.
He said high-risk, repeat or predatory offenders who continue to pose a threat could be subject to wider community notification, while information on lower-risk offenders should remain restricted.
"The principal objective must be crime prevention and the protection of vulnerable members of society rather than punishment beyond the sentence imposed by the courts.
"A tiered model represents the most balanced approach. Risk assessment should be evidence-based and periodically reviewed by qualified professionals," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Sundramoorthy said the recent  case in Shah Alam had intensified public concern  about existing child protection mechanisms, but stressed that any public registry should  contain only individuals convicted by a court and not those merely under investigation.
Sundramoorthy said greater access could enhance public safety by helping parents, educational institutions and community organisations protect children and adolescents from individuals assessed as posing a continuing risk.
However, he cautioned that unrestricted disclosure could lead to vigilantism, harassment, mistaken identity and social ostracism, while undermining rehabilitation and potentially increasing risk factors associated with re-offending.
"Mandatory screening against the registry should become a legal requirement for all organisations employing people who have direct and regular contact with minors.
"Such checks should also be conducted periodically rather than only during initial recruitment," he said, adding that safeguards should include reference verification, continuous monitoring and behavioural reporting mechanisms.
Sundramoorthy said Malaysia should consider a hybrid model combining mandatory institutional screening with targeted public disclosure for high-risk offenders, alongside stronger information-sharing between police, education authorities, child protection agencies and licensed employers.
In 2019, the Women, Family and Community Development Ministry set up the Sexual Crime Registry System which contained the names of 3,000 offenders convicted  of sexual offences against children.
The list, however, was not made public. To access the database, one has to fill up a form, stating the purpose, and provide the identity card number of the person.
If the application is done at a state Welfare Department, the results can be obtained immediately but if done at a district-level office,   it will take five working days.
Malaysians Against Rape, Assault and Snatch Theft  founder Dave Avran said the registry must be made fully public.
"Every parent, neighbour and member of our community deserves to know when there is a proven risk to our children and vulnerable adults living nearby."
He said public access would help communities make informed  decisions when choosing schools, arranging childcare, organising community events and supervising interactions involving children.
He acknowledged concerns over vigilantism, misuse of data and harm to offenders' families, but said these could be addressed by limiting information to convicted offenders who meet specific thresholds and imposing penalties for doxxing, harassment and profiteering from registry information.
Avran also proposed transparent appeal procedures, periodic reviews and pathways for removal in cases of demonstrable rehabilitation, saying the registry should be complemented by improved offender supervision, victim support services and prevention programmes.
"Making the registry public is not about punishment beyond the law. It is about equipping citizens with facts that protect vulnerable people and deter future harm."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Air Astana mulls KL return
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Air Astana remains interested in returning to Kuala Lumpur as the Kazakhstan's national carrier continues evaluating new destinations and expanding its international network.
Air Astana Group chief executive officer (CEO) Ibrahim Canliel said Kuala Lumpur remains a destination that Air Astana would like to serve again although other opportunities currently rank higher within its network development plans.
"Kuala Lumpur is just outside the range of our narrowbody aircraft. So, it does require a widebody (aircraft) operation, of which we have limited capacity.
"We are still looking at Kuala Lumpur as a destination that we would like to return to. However, it is relatively lower in the ranking because the flight time is long, it is  outside narrowbody range, and at the moment the relative profitability of other destinations is higher."
He spoke with Business Times in an interview on the sidelines of the International Air Transport Association (IATA) 82nd annual general meeting recently.
Air Astana first launched direct services between Almaty and Kuala Lumpur in October 2009.
The route operated for over a decade before being suspended in 2020 following the Covid-19 pandemic.
Canliel said medium-haul markets, particularly in China, have been performing strongly for the airline.
"What we have seen this year is that the medium-haul markets, for example in China, are performing extremely well. The Gulf was also performing extremely well until we stopped flying there," he added.
China has become one of Air Astana's fastest growing markets this year.
The airline launched direct flights between Astana and Guangzhou on June 2, complementing the existing Almaty-Guangzhou service that has operated since March 2025.
The carrier also launched a new route between Almaty and Shanghai at the end of March this year, expanding its network in China to include Beijing, Guangzhou, Shanghai, Sanya, Urumqi and Yining.
Air Astana, which is part of the Air Astana Group, had also expanded its presence in the Gulf in recent years with Dubai among its key destinations in the region.
The carrier has since announced the resumption of Dubai services with flights between Almaty and Dubai restarting from June 20 and Astana-Dubai services resuming from July 10.
The flights will be operated via Pakistan due to the closure of Iranian airspace, while frequencies will be gradually restored over the coming weeks.
Air Astana is also benefitting from growing transit traffic through Kazakhstan.
The airline group reported a 65 per cent increase in international transit passenger traffic in the first quarter of 2026 compared with the same period last year.
The group said total transit traffic exceeded 137,000 passengers since the beginning of 2026, while international transit traffic surpassed 48,000 passengers.
The increase in the transit traffic was driven by the group's network expansion and the reallocation of capacity from the Middle East to Seoul, Bangkok, Phuket, Phu Quoc, Male, Istanbul, Delhi, Tashkent and Tbilisi.
Canliel said there is a shift in connectivity from Asia to Central Asia and the Caucasus, from Europe to Central Asia and the Caucasus as well as between Europe and Asia.
"We see both point-to-point traffic increasing as well as connecting traffic within the mega markets connecting into Central Asia and the Caucasus and also connecting east and west.
"We do anticipate that when the dust settles there will be a different landscape that will be sustained," Canliel, who was appointed Air Astana Group CEO on April 1 this year.
He said the airline is also seeing increased interest from travellers using Kazakhstan as a gateway to neighbouring countries.
"Many travellers are visiting Kazakhstan as a destination in its own right. Others are flying to Kazakhstan and then connecting onwards to places such as Georgia and Uzbekistan."
Canliel said Air Astana continues to pursue expansion opportunities despite some airlines scaling back capacity amid industry challenges.
"This year, we are looking at introducing up to 15 new routes. The ambition is there. The counter-cyclical behaviour is in place.
"We know that many airlines are reducing capacity and cutting frequencies. To the contrary, we are actually expanding capacity in Central Asia, Southeast Asia and Europe," he said.
Air Astana is scheduled to receive its B787 aircraft in September and November this year as well as in mid-2027.
The airline is also studying potential services to the United States, although Canliel said several regulatory and operational requirements must first be addressed.
"The United States is one of the destinations that we are looking at. For us to be able to fly to the US, a number of things need to happen.
"There is a certain routing that we need to take in order to be able to use the Great Circle route. There are a number of clearances that need to be achieved. If and when those are resolved, we will of course be looking at flights to the US," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Why the world's chokepoints still govern the global economy
Article:
MAPS never stopped mattering. We merely convinced ourselves they did.
For almost three decades, globalisation encouraged governments and businesses alike to believe that technology, markets and just-in-time supply chains had conquered geography.
Production shifted to wherever costs were lowest, capital flowed with unprecedented ease, and the physical map appeared increasingly subordinate to the digital economy.
Recent events have exposed that assumption as one of the defining strategic miscalculations of our time.
Around one-fifth of globally traded crude oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz, while the Suez Canal carries roughly one-third of global container traffic.
Meanwhile, the Strait of Malacca remains one of the world's busiest maritime corridors and the principal maritime gateway linking East Asia with the Indian Ocean, the Middle East and Europe.
These are not merely shipping routes; they are the arteries through which the global economy breathes.
Few countries have a greater stake in this return of geography than Malaysia.
Situated astride the Strait of Malacca, Malaysia's prosperity has always depended as much on its strategic location as on its natural resources.
At a time when multinational corporations are reassessing supply chains and governments are recalibrating national security strategies, Malaysia occupies one of the most strategically valuable positions in the emerging geoeconomic landscape.
More importantly, these waterways are no longer merely geographical features.
They function as economic institutions, shaping commodity prices, insurance premiums, investment decisions and supply-chain resilience in much the same way that financial markets, payment systems and capital markets influence the global economy.
Their uninterrupted operation underpins international commerce; their disruption reverberates across industries and continents. This represents one of the defining transformations of the contemporary international political economy.
If the first article in this series argued that geopolitical uncertainty has become the new currency of power, this second proposition follows naturally: geography determines where that currency is minted, traded and priced.
Maritime chokepoints have become the exchanges through which geopolitical risk is transmitted into the global economy.
When Geography Becomes An Economic Institution
The world's major maritime chokepoints - the Strait of Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb, the Suez Canal, the Strait of Malacca and increasingly the Taiwan Strait - should no longer be viewed simply as narrow waterways connecting oceans.
They are strategic economic institutions that sustain the movement of energy, manufactured goods, agricultural commodities, semiconductors and intermediate inputs upon which the global economy depends.
When these arteries function normally, their significance is often overlooked.
Their value becomes apparent only when they are disrupted.
The tensions surrounding the Strait of Hormuz in 2026 provided a vivid reminder of this reality.
Even after military tensions subsided and shipping resumed, commercial confidence recovered far more slowly.
Shipping companies, insurers and commodity traders continued to factor geopolitical uncertainty into freight rates, insurance costs and operational decisions.
The episode demonstrated that markets respond not merely to actual conflict, but to the probability that disruption may occur.
Risk itself has become an economic variable.
The same logic applies to the Red Sea. Repeated attacks on commercial vessels forced many shipping lines to reroute around the Cape of Good Hope, extending voyages by thousands of nautical miles, increasing fuel consumption, reducing fleet availability and raising transportation costs across multiple industries.
A relatively narrow maritime corridor generated consequences that reached manufacturers in Asia, retailers in Europe and consumers around the world.
The Suez Canal reinforces the same lesson. The grounding of the Ever Given in 2021 demonstrated that a single incident could interrupt hundreds of billions of dollars in global trade within days.
No military confrontation was required. The episode simply exposed how heavily global commerce depends upon a handful of critical geographical corridors.
The Taiwan Strait illustrates that geography today is no longer about the movement of oil alone. It also underpins the movement of semiconductors, advanced electronics and the digital infrastructure upon which the modern economy increasingly depends.
Any sustained disruption would reverberate far beyond Northeast Asia, affecting industries ranging from artificial intelligence and automotive manufacturing to telecommunications and advanced defence technologies.
In every case, geography becomes economics.
From Efficiency to Resilience
For much of the post-Cold War period, efficiency defined globalisation. Companies optimised production around cost, speed and scale.
Inventories were minimised, production dispersed across multiple jurisdictions and redundancy was viewed as inefficiency.
Today, resilience increasingly defines geoeconomics.
Corporations now evaluate investment destinations using criteria once associated primarily with national security planners: political stability, maritime security, alliance networks, energy resilience, logistics redundancy and strategic access to reliable shipping routes.
Supply-chain resilience has become as important as production efficiency.
The objective is no longer simply to produce at the lowest possible cost, but to ensure that production can continue during periods of geopolitical uncertainty.
This represents a profound transformation in how businesses assess comparative advantage. Geography has once again become a commercial asset rather than merely a physical characteristic.
For Malaysia, this shift presents both opportunity and responsibility. The country's strategic location, established manufacturing ecosystem, sophisticated financial sector and longstanding commitment to an open trading system position it favourably as firms diversify supply chains and seek trusted partners within the Indo-Pacific.
Malaysia's diplomatic tradition further reinforces this advantage.
By maintaining constructive relations with major powers while remaining firmly anchored within Asean, Malaysia has cultivated a reputation for strategic balance and policy predictability.
In an increasingly fragmented international environment, these qualities enhance investor confidence and strengthen the country's role as a regional commercial hub.
Yet geography alone is never sufficient.
Strategic location must be complemented by efficient ports, world-class logistics, institutional credibility, regulatory certainty, digital infrastructure and sustained investment in human capital.
Geography creates opportunity; policy determines whether that opportunity is realised.
The broader lesson extends well beyond Malaysia.
The 21st century is witnessing the return of geography as a central organising principle of international economics.
Artificial intelligence, cloud computing, advanced manufacturing, renewable energy and digital commerce all depend upon physical infrastructure - ports, undersea cables, shipping lanes, logistics hubs and maritime chokepoints.
Even the digital economy ultimately rests upon tangible geographical foundations.
The countries that prosper in the decades ahead will not necessarily be those with the largest economies or the strongest militaries. They will be those that best understand how geography, economics and diplomacy reinforce one another.
History has not ended. Geography has returned - and with it, the enduring reality that control over the world's arteries of commerce remains one of the oldest and most effective forms of strategic power.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Venezuela quake deaths pass 3,000, attention turns to mourning, burials
Article:
CATIA LA MAR, Venezuela: Deaths from Venezuela's devastating twin earthquakes rose to at least 3,342 on Sunday as officials began to bury dozens of bodies left unidentified 11 days after the disaster.
The June 24 double shocks, one of Latin America's worst quake disasters, toppled scores of buildings in the La Guaira coastal area north of Caracas and left thousands of people missing.
As international rescue teams packed up and ended attempts to find survivors, attention shifted to mourning those lost and burying the remains that families have recovered from the ruins.
In a secluded area of La Esperanza cemetery in La Guaira, gravediggers buried more than 150 bodies still unidentified since the earthquakes, AFP journalists saw.
A line of simple white crosses with small bouquets at their foot marked a long row of individual graves. Each one had the same date of death: June 24, 2026.
Two excavators were working to dig more graves in the light brown earth.
"We are first and foremost overcome with grief," said local resident Eli Zavala, who was helping with the burials.
"We started here on July 25th, the very next day, to do all the work...so that all those people could have dignified burials."
According to updated official figures on Sunday, at least 3,342 people died and another 16,700 were injured.
With nearly 200 buildings totally collapsed, most of them in La Guaira, more than 17,000 people have been left homeless and are sleeping in shelters and temporary camps.
The government has not given any figure for those still missing, but the UN estimates that as many as 50,000 people may still be unaccounted for after the shocks.
Many families are still trying to search for relatives in the rubble.
"I've lost track of the days. You lose your mind, but I'm not leaving here because I know he's there," said Zuly, looking for her son in Catia la Mar district.
She now sleeps in a plaza near where he worked.
"I found his motorcycle, I found his helmet. He's there, God willing, alive. If not, at least I can find him, see him...I'm not leaving here without my son."
- 'NO SOCIAL UNREST' -
Even before the quakes, Venezuela had been struggling with economic crisis and political upheaval that left infrastructure and health services depleted.
The UN estimates the quakes caused US$6.7 billion in damage - equivalent to six percent of Venezuela's GDP.
The damaged international airport serving Caracas is still closed to commercial flights.
Soon after the quakes, many Venezuelans complained they were left alone to dig for families in the ruins and criticized the government's response until international teams arrived.
Interim President Delcy Rodriguez has defended the government reaction, saying thousands of public officials and rescue teams were dispatched.
On Sunday, she dismissed concerns about upheaval over the quakes.
"There will be no social unrest here, what we have here is deep social solidarity," Rodriguez said during a military ceremony marking the country's independence day.
Across Caracas and La Guaira, many were focussed instead on Sunday services in churches to remember those lost and still missing.
On the campus of Venezuela Central University in Caracas, dozens of people gathered around a large Venezuela flag surrounded by candles for a vigil.
"I've met with couples who have lost both their children, or two of their three. It's very painful," Father Rafael Troconis told AFP in La Guaira.
"You try to offer support as much as you can. You want to be close to those who are suffering. You notice a lot of sadness and despair."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Reports of 'major damages' from super typhoon on US island Rota
Article:
SAIPAN: Authorities on Rota said Monday they had received reports from residents of "major damages" as Super Typhoon Bavi roared over the US Pacific island.
"We are hanging in there. We are experiencing heavy winds and flooding here at Rota's MOC. Some people already reporting major damages," the Rota mayor's operations center public information officer Lou Rosario said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dengue survivors turn painful lessons into action
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: What three dengue patients thought was a normal fever quickly became a painful ordeal, leaving them bedridden, exhausted and, in one case, with liver complications.
Their experiences come as health authorities continue urging Malaysians to eliminate mosquito breeding sites, particularly stagnant water.
TV3 cameraman Mohammed Fairuz Yazeer, 35, sought treatment at a Kota Kinabalu private hospital  about a week of fever, chills, diarrhoea, loss of appetite, severe joint pain and rashes.
He had learnt that a neighbour had tested positive for dengue.
"I became worried, so I went to the hospital the next day. I tested positive and was told I was close to entering the critical phase. My liver was also affected."
Fairuz spent four days in hospital before gradually recovering. He credited staying hydrated with helping his recovery.
"I really thought it was just a normal fever. I didn't expect it to get that serious."
For veterinary medicine student Muhammad Amirul Shauqat Azaha, 25, the illness struck without warning while he was in a dengue red zone.
He said it had happened while he was staying at his rented house in Pengkalan Chepa, Kelantan, a dengue hotspot.
He initially mistook it for a normal fever.
"I was perfectly fine in the morning, then suddenly I started shivering and felt very weak. I went home and lay down because I thought it was just a fever."
Severe headaches, a high fever and a complete loss of appetite left Amirul unable to carry out even basic tasks such as going to the kitchen or toilet.
After three days without improvement, he sought medical attention.
"I realised something was wrong when I didn't recover like I usually would from a normal fever. Usually, I would feel better after two days, but this time it kept getting worse."
He later learnt that his neighbourhood was a dengue hotspot, with several relatives infected at the same time.
Following a blood test, Amirul was advised to recover at home, where he spent two weeks under his mother's care while waiting for his platelet and white blood cell counts to return to normal levels.
"I didn't expect dengue to hit this hard. I wish I had known earlier."
Engineer Shukrie Sayuti, 34, also dismissed his prolonged fever while living alone at Lagoon Perdana Sunway.
"The fatigue was so bad I couldn't work. Just getting up to get food was difficult, so I mostly stayed in bed and rested."
Shukrie sought treatment only after noticing red spots on his body, by which time his condition had begun to stabilise.
"I thank God I survived. I should have gone earlier instead of assuming it was just a fever."
All three patients said dengue was far more severe than they had expected. They urged the public not to ignore persistent fever, headaches and body aches.
Amirul said the experience had changed his perception of the disease and reinforced the importance of early action and prevention.
"Clean your surroundings and don't wait until someone gets sick. Dengue is not just a fever — it can change your life."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK tightens rules on overseas political donations to stop 'dodgy funding'
Article:
LONDON: Britain tightened its rules on overseas political donations on Monday, as part of efforts to stop foreign money influencing elections, and as concerns swirl around what Housing Minister Steve Reed called "dodgy funding."
Last year, the government ordered a review into foreign financial interference in politics after a former politician in Reform UK was jailed for taking bribes to make pro-Russia speeches and statements.
The review found Britain faced a persistent problem of foreign countries, including Russia, China and Iran, trying to influence and undermine the country's democracy, prompting the government to introduce the new rules.
They come as the leader of the anti-immigration Reform UK party, Nigel Farage, is under investigation by the parliamentary standards watchdog over whether he should have declared a £5 million (US$6.68 million) donation from a Thailand-based cryptocurrency billionaire, which was given before Farage said he would stand as a member of parliament.
Britain's Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government said on Monday that from now on political candidates would be required to declare donations of over £2,230 received prior to becoming a candidate and prove any pre-candidacy funding had come from legitimate sources.
"By holding overseas donors to tougher standards and requiring candidates to prove where their funding comes from, we are taking world-leading action to protect the integrity of our elections and tackle the threats we face from abroad," Reed said in a statement.
The tougher rules also mean individuals moving to the UK from overseas now have to live permanently in the UK for a year before they can make a political donation of £100,000 or more, while donations from companies are to be assessed against past post-tax profits rather than revenue, helping to ensure only legitimate UK-linked businesses can donate.
These rules build on those announced in March, which capped donations by Britons living abroad at £100,000 per year, and introduced a ban on crypto donations until a system to regulate them effectively is established.
Farage's Reform UK, which has topped national opinion polls for over a year, has said no rules were broken over the donation to Farage from crypto investor Christopher Harborne, who provided about two-thirds of Reform's funding last year, according to Electoral Commission data. On Sunday, Farage was referred to parliament's standards watchdog after a report that he failed to declare some other benefits.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: When dengue turns deadly: Doctors warn against lowering your guard [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Blood soaked through a hospital bed as doctors rushed to save a dengue patient whose condition deteriorated within hours.
The woman's platelet count suddenly crashed during the disease's critical phase, triggering severe bleeding.
As her haemoglobin level fell rapidly, doctors scrambled to stabilise her with repeated blood transfusions.
For Dr Wendy Chong Pei Wen, a consultant internal medicine physician at Columbia Asia Hospital Petaling Jaya, the case remains a stark reminder that dengue can become life threatening with little warning.
"Her haemoglobin fell from 10 to eight and we had to rush blood products.
"After the first transfusion, her haemo-globin dropped again, so she required another transfusion.
"In total, we gave four packs of blood. Fortunately, she recovered because she was admitted early."
She said not every dengue patient was as fortunate.
As dengue cases continue to rise nationwide, doctors say hospitals continue to treat a steady stream of patients and warn that containing the disease will require more than periodic fogging campaigns.
They say the increase reflects a combination of changing weather patterns, rapid urbanisation, shifts in circulating dengue virus serotypes and persistent mosquito breeding sites, underscoring the need for stronger public awareness and sustained community action.
One of the biggest misconceptions, Dr Chong said, is that patients believe they are out of danger once the fever subsides.
In fact, the period between the fifth and seventh day of illness is often when doctors become most concerned, as platelet counts can fall sharply, blood pressure may drop and the risk of severe bleeding increases.
"Dengue is unpredictable. It's a dynamic infection.
"You may feel well now, but that doesn't mean complications won't develop later," she said.
She urged anyone experiencing persistent fever, extreme fatigue, persistent vomiting or difficulty drinking fluids to seek medical attention without delay.
[[nid:1481421]]
Dr Sai Shankar Gunasingam, a consultant physician and internal medicine specialist at Mahameru International Medical Centre here, said climate change, heavy rainfall and rapid urbanisation were creating conditions that favoured dengue transmission.
Hot weather accelerates the hatching of Aedes mosquito eggs, while torrential rain creates stagnant water where mosquitoes could breed.
Meanwhile, expanding cities, high-rise housing and construction sites also provide more breeding habitats while bringing more people into close proximity.
Dr Sai said densely populated condominiums were particularly vulnerable because Aedes mosquitoes typically remained within a limited flight range.
"If one person in a condominium is infected, many more people in the same area can become infected because the mosquito stays within that vicinity."
He warned against assuming that dry containers were free of mosquito eggs.
"People think that because the container is dry, there are no eggs. That's wrong.
"The eggs remain attached to the container, so it must be washed thoroughly instead of simply emptied," he said, noting that Aedes eggs could survive for months before hatching once exposed to water again.
From a broader public health perspective, Professor Dr Jenarun Jelip, a public health medicine specialist and infectious disease epidemiologist, said no single factor explained the rise in dengue cases.
Instead, he said the increase reflected interacting epidemiological, environmental and virological factors.
Among the most significant is an apparent shift in the predominant circulating dengue virus serotype, which could leave many people susceptible despite having contracted dengue previously.
"Infection with one dengue virus serotype provides lifelong immunity only against that particular serotype," said Dr Jenarun.
"When another serotype becomes dominant, many individuals remain susceptible to infection despite previous dengue illness, while those infected with a different serotype for a second time face a greater risk of developing severe dengue."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia records sharp rise in dengue cases and deaths
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Dengue cases have increased significantly this year, while deaths have risen sharply.
Health authorities, however, said the outbreak remains under control.
Malaysia reported 38,854 dengue cases up to June 27 — up 29.1 per cent from the same period last year.
The Health Ministry said deaths increased from 18 to 30, which it attributed to the dominant dengue virus serotype shifting from DENV-2 to DENV-3, alongside urbanisation, climate factors, persistent mosquito breeding sites and increased population movement.
"In terms of dengue-related mortality, 30 deaths have been reported up to the same date in 2026 compared with 18 deaths in 2025, representing an increase of 66.7 per cent," a ministry spokesman said.
"The ministry does not base its public health interventions on fear, but rather on data-driven assessments and scientific evidence," he said when asked if the situation could worsen.
The spokesman said the ministry was monitoring the situation and had activated surveillance and response systems at all levels.
While cases have increased, he said, epidemiological assessments showed no unusual deviation from the country's dengue transmission patterns, describing the disease as cyclical.
The rise, he said, was concentrated in several states where rapid development and construction had created conditions conducive to mosquito breeding.
State and district health authorities have been instructed to intensify vector-control operations, including targeted fogging, in priority areas.
Targeted Outdoor Residual Spraying (TORS) has been expanded in selected persistent hotspots, while community-based programmes under the Komuniti Bebas Denggi (KomBeD) initiative have been intensified.
[[nid:1481494]]
The spokesman said the ministry was strengthening digital surveillance systems to allow earlier detection of transmission patterns and faster response.
It is integrating predictive tools using climate data, rainfall, temperature and geospatial mapping to identify high-risk areas.
He said Malaysia maintained close surveillance cooperation with regional partners, including Singapore and Thailand, as dengue remains a shared public health challenge across Southeast Asia.
Countries in the region face similar ecological and climate conditions that support transmission, and dengue patterns remain broadly cyclical across borders.
However, Malaysia's current incidence rate, measured per population, is higher than that of both Singapore and Thailand, although all countries continue to report cases within manageable ranges.
The spokesman said the ministry continued to exchange surveillance data and best practices with Asean counterparts, stressing that dengue control requires sustained regional cooperation rather than country-by-country comparison.
He said dengue prevention could not rely solely on government intervention.
"It requires individual responsibility to eliminate breeding sites," he said, urging Malaysians to carry out weekly checks to remove stagnant water and to seek early treatment if symptoms appear.
The ministry said existing resources were sufficient to manage the current situation and scale up interventions where needed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: BN, PH promising 'stability'
Article:
JOHOR BARU:   As campaigning for the Johor election enters its final stretch, Barisan Nasional and Pakatan Harapan are going all out to sell  their competing visions of political stability.
For BN, stability means continuity under caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi, backed by the state's strong economic performance and development plans.
PH, meanwhile, is pitching a different proposition — that Johor stands to gain more with a state government aligned with Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's administration in Putrajaya, promising closer federal-state cooperation that could accelerate development in Johor.
One example is how close cooperation between the federal and state governments could help create more quality, high-paying jobs, reducing the need for people  to seek employment   outstation.
PH election director Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said  while Johor has recorded the highest gross domestic product growth in the country, this has not fully translated into high-paying jobs.
"Johor's development plans, if combined with the strength of the federal government, I am confident the state will become a place that generates good-quality, high-value jobs.
"People in the state will no longer need to cross the Causeway every day in search of a living," he said at the launch of the PH election manifesto.
Apart from efforts to create more jobs, PH is also offering pledges such as assistance for first-time homebuyers and health protection, in its bid to win and consolidate support in the election.
BN, through state chairman Onn Hafiz, has been consistent in calling for continuity in the hope of securing another landslide majority in this election or potentially increase its seat tally.
With a fresh mandate, BN aims to continue all welfare programmes, economic initiatives and state development efforts outlined in its long-term Johor Maju 2030 vision.
As polling day draws closer, both BN and PH — the main contenders in the polls — are racing against time to promote their visions of political stability.
The weekend also saw top leaders from both coalitions descend on the state to lend support and campaign for their candidates.
On many occasions, PH has called on the  people to come out and cast their votes in hopes of increasing the voter turnout.
The coalition believes that a higher turnout could help it to, if not produce an upset, at least dent BN's efforts to secure another landslide victory.
Not to be outdone, BN chairman and Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi also spent the weekend campaigning, joining state leaders and candidates in a series of events to reinforce the coalition's campaign machinery and consolidate support.
Universiti Malaya Department of Political Science, Public Administration and Development Studies senior lecturer Dr Mohammad Tawfik Yaakub said although the first week shows BN is in a comfortable position, PH still has a chance to reduce the gap in the second week of campaigning.
"This, however, depends on PH's ability to mobilise urban voters, non-Malay voters, young voters and fence-sitters.
"If turnout increases in urban and mixed areas, PH could defend key seats and even narrow the gap.
"However, if turnout is low, the advantage typically goes to parties with stronger 'traditional' machinery. In the Johor context, this clearly favours BN."
Ilham Centre researcher Mujibu Abd Muis said BN cannot afford to be complacent as being a frontrunner does not guarantee a large victory if there are weaknesses in  grassroots campaigning.
"In elections involving many marginal seats, even small changes in turnout or shifts of just a few percentage points in votes can be enough to alter outcomes of the polls.
"The second week of the campaign is the decisive phase. BN still has structural advantages, but PH has tactical opportunities.
"As such, the focus of this election is no longer simply about who wins, but how far BN can maintain its dominance and how far PH can narrow the gap and defend its seats."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kg Istana all for digital push to boost ecotourism
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
BATU PAHAT: Residents of Kampung Istana in Penggaram here welcome government efforts to develop the village's ecotourism potential through digital marketing training and improved Internet connectivity.
Village Development and Security Committee chairman Abd Jalil Ali said the Communications Ministry's initiative would greatly benefit the village's 1,300 residents.
He said although Kampung Istana is located in the heart of Batu Pahat, it has retained its identity as a traditional village since its establishment in the 1910s, and has unique attractions due to its proximity to recreational areas such as Bukit Banang and Bukit Soga.
"We are excited that Kampung Istana has been selected as the ministry's Madani adopted village this year.
"With support in digital marketing training, better Internet access and upgraded facilities, we hope it can be better known as an ecotourism destination.
"Residents here can also learn how to promote the village's products, activities and uniqueness through digital platforms," he said at Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil's meet-and-greet session with Kampung Istana residents on Saturday.
Earlier, Fahmi said Kampung Istana had the potential to become an ecotourism destination, supported by digital marketing training for entrepreneurs and better Internet access.
Jalil said Kampung Istana's selection as a Madani adopted village has benefited residents through the upgrade of several public facilities, including the community hall.
The programme has also created opportunities for the community to carry out more social, welfare and development activities, he added.
Meanwhile, Persatuan Muafakat Belia Kampung Istana chairman Mohamad Afnan Amran said better Internet connectivity would allow youths to seize digital economic opportunities without leaving their hometown.
"If the Internet is more stable and there is digital marketing training, we can help promote the village, local products and community activities to outsiders."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dengue survivors turn painful lessons into action
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
PETALING JAYA: A single puddle is all it takes.
As dengue cases continue to rise, residents in parts of the Klang Valley are stepping up efforts to eliminate mosquito breeding sites, fearing a disease that can strike without warning and leave families devastated.
In Seksyen 17, Petaling Jaya, housewife Dara Zalia, 48, no longer ignores even the smallest collection of water around her home.
Each week, she checks flower pots, clears blocked drains and tidies her garden to prevent Aedes mosquitoes from breeding.
Having suffered dengue herself and watched both her husband and daughter contract the disease, she said the experience changed her outlook.
"I make sure there is no stagnant water in the flower pots, the drains are clear and the garden is always clean. I don't want my family to go through dengue again."
A few streets away, student Siti Maryam, 26, said dengue prevention had become part of her family's routine through regular clean-ups and measures to reduce mosquito exposure.
Although she considers the neighbourhood relatively well maintained, preventing dengue requires collective effort.
"I think young people are aware of dengue, but not everyone takes prevention seriously. Some would rather spend their time doing other things than help keep their surroundings clean."
For lecturer Zunaibi Abdullah, 62, the threat remains personal.
Having previously contracted dengue, he recently underwent a blood test after developing symptoms doctors suspected could be linked to another infection.
His earlier bout left him suffering severe back pain, fatigue, lethargy and constant thirst.
Their vigilance comes as local authorities intensify efforts to curb transmission through inspections, fogging operations and public awareness campaigns.
The Shah Alam City Council said hotspots were locations where outbreaks persist for more than 30 days, triggering inspections to identify breeding sites, fogging to eliminate adult mosquitoes and larviciding to destroy larvae.
The council said enforcement action was also taken under the Local Government Act 1976 and the Prevention of Mosquito-Borne Diseases Act 1975, while operations were being conducted with other agencies to tackle illegal dumping and maintain cleanliness.
However, weather conditions, stagnant water, illegal dumping and limited cooperation from some residents continue to hamper efforts.
Taman Batu Muda Residents Association chairman Rahmad Basid said cooperation between residents and local authorities had helped reduce dengue cases in the neighbourhood.
"Dengue prevention is a shared responsibility. The authorities can carry out fogging and inspections, but residents must also play their part by eliminating stagnant water and keeping their surroundings clean."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Merdeka 118 EV hub opens
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Yinson GreenTech has launched a 32-bay alternating current (AC) electric vehicle (EV) charging hub at Menara Merdeka 118. The project was developed in collaboration with PNB Merdeka Ventures Sdn Bhd.
Operated by chargEV — Yinson GreenTech's electric mobility arm — the facility is Malaysia's largest single-site AC charging hub, featuring 22-kilowatt (kW) chargers.
"This collaboration reflects the need for a charging provider that can match the scale of Merdeka 118 and serve tenants, visitors and the community," the company said.
The next phase of the collaboration will introduce direct current (DC) fast chargers at the adjacent 118 Mall, which is scheduled to open in August.
Yinson GreenTech chief executive officer Louisa Brady said the hub aims to expand EV charging access at key urban locations.
"With 32 charging bays at Menara Merdeka 118, our chargEV network represents one of the largest single-site AC charging hubs in Malaysia.
"It is designed to improve reliability, accessibility and the charging experience for EV users."
PNB Merdeka chief executive officer Datuk Izwan Ibrahim said the charging infrastructure is part of Merdeka 118 sustainability initiatives and supports growing demand for EV facilities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: N. Korea's Kim oversees weapons test of repaired warship
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
SEOUL: North Korean leader Kim Jong-un oversaw weapons tests of the 5,000-tonne destroyer Kang Kon, including cruise missile launches and "electronic warfare means", state media reported yesterday.
The naval vessel was notably the one that tipped over last year during its launch ceremony, but was later repaired.
The tests took place on Friday, the state-run Korean Central News Agency (KCNA) said, less than two weeks after another 5,000-tonne destroyer, the Choe Hyon, entered military service.
During that commissioning ceremony, Kim vowed to equip his navy with nuclear weapons and develop new 10,000-tonne warships.
After the weapons test on Friday, Kim "gave an instruction to complete the trial process of the destroyer in a responsible manner and commission it for the Navy within two months".
A photo released by KCNA shows Kim, wearing a yellow hat, watching a weapons test from a coastal observation point, flanked by officials.
Another photo shows Kang Kon firing a missile at sea, with a plume of smoke rising from the launch.
While reiterating the need to strengthen the country's "war deterrent", Kim also pledged to demonstrate North Korea's determination to possess "absolute power", KCNA said. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Venezuela quake survivor 'reborn' after eight days in rubble
Article:
CARACAS: Hernan Gil was working his shift as a security guard in the basement of a building in Venezuela on the afternoon of June 24 when he felt the earth shake.
The first tremor was short, Gil recounted in an interview with AFP from the hospital room where he was recovering after being rescued from the depths of a collapsed building.
Gil became a symbol of hope for Venezuela after he survived eight days of being buried alive by the double earthquake that devastated the north of the Caribbean country, leaving nearly 3,000 dead.
"The second one was incredibly strong," said the 43-year-old, who remembers freezing inside his guard booth, overcome with nerves.
He heard a neighbor in the parking lot say it was an earthquake, and then "everything collapsed."
Gil felt everything crumble and stones hit the back of his head and eye.
"I lost consciousness for a moment. When I woke up, everything was dark...From then on, everything was uncertain."
Gil couldn't see anything or hear anyone. He tried calling the neighbor he had last seen before being swallowed by the earth, "but nothing."
"And at that moment, despair overwhelmed me," said Gil, who began shouting for help, but received no response.
In the darkness, partially kneeling, gasping for air and practically immobile, he suffered aftershock after aftershock.
"I felt like the wall was completely crushing me."
- 'I prayed a lot' -
Despite the traumatic experience, Gil appeared in good spirits 72 hours after being freed from the hole where he was buried beneath a collapsing eight-story building.
Seated in an armchair, dressed in a blue hospital gown, with his left arm in a sling, the security guard said his faith sustained him during the harrowing hours.
"I prayed a lot. I cried out to God, and I said, 'My God, why me? Why like this? Please, at least let me see my children.'"
Trapped in an uncomfortable position, Gil tried to lie on either side of his hole, but he couldn't sleep.
The rocks he had fallen on bruised his legs, his nose bled, and his right eye was swollen and red, although he didn't realize that at the time.
In those lonely hours, he thought of his wife, Gusbimar Gonzalez, who was outside looking for any sign of life.
He also thought of his children and his deceased father.
"So many memories came flooding back," he said.
Gil lost track of time, but it was on the third day that he heard footsteps, "but very far away."
He began to shout and call for help, and finally, he heard a response.
Gil remembers, smiling, that at that moment he thought, "Oh my God, there's a step. There's a glimmer of hope."
But then began a dramatic rescue that Gil described as "a fierce struggle."
- 'I was reborn' -
While rescue teams from seven countries fought to reach Gil, keep him hydrated, and maintain his spirits, the 43-year-old felt the walls continue to shift, trapping him more and more.
But when two rescuers from Chile and the United States finally reached him after more than three days of complex operations, Gil couldn't bring himself to rejoice.
"The hardest part was getting out," recalled Gil, whose legs had been entangled in a chair.
Gil has no doubt that it was a life-changing miracle.
"I was reborn!" he said.
Now in the hospital, Gil managed to talk to his children via video call and can't wait to go home. But the doctors haven't given him a discharge date.
He sleeps more easily now, but sometimes the harrowing moments he experienced underground wake him.
Accompanied day and night by his wife, Gil isn't quite sure what comes next in his new life.
For now, all he knows is that he wants to celebrate his son's birthday on July 15th, take some long-awaited vacations at the beach, and that he'll never work in a basement again.
pr/pma/jgc
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Infernos devastate forests as Europe's temperatures rise again
Article:
BARCELONA: Wildfires raged across southern Europe on Sunday, forcing thousands of people to evacuate their homes and prompting Tour de France officials to ban spectators from a stage of the race.
Hundreds of firefighters are battling blazes that have devastated more than 19,000 hectares (42,000 acres) of land – an area more than twice the size of Manhattan – across Portugal, Spain, France and Greece.
And temperatures are on the rise again, predicted to reach 40C in parts of a region still suffering the aftermath of last week's record-breaking heatwave.
Some 10,500 people were being evacuated from their homes near the city of Perpignan in southwestern France as firefighters tried to control a blaze that has devoured 1,650 hectares, authorities said.
"The fire came within 300 metres of the houses. We were taken aback by how fast it spread, it was staggering – bordering on panic," said Patrice, a 53-year-old resident of the vilage of Trevillach, who did not wish to give his surname.
